verses.text: The LORD appears to Abraham at Mamre. Three men are standing nearby. In the heat of the day, Abraham runs from his tent to meet them — Eastern hospitality at full speed, even at 99 years old.
The LORD appears to Abraham at Mamre. Three men are standing nearby. In the heat of the day, Abraham runs from his tent to meet them — Eastern hospitality at full speed, even at 99 years old.
He bows, calls one of them my Lord, and begs them to stay. A little water, a piece of bread. The wording is humble — but he is about to prepare a feast. Generosity often understates itself.
He bows, calls one of them my Lord, and begs them to stay. A little water, a piece of bread. The wording is humble — but he is about to prepare a feast. Generosity often understates itself.
He bows, calls one of them my Lord, and begs them to stay. A little water, a piece of bread. The wording is humble — but he is about to prepare a feast. Generosity often understates itself.

Abraham hurries Sarah to bake cakes from three measures of flour — that's enough bread for forty. He runs to the herd, selects a tender calf, and stands while they eat under the tree. The patriarch waits on his guests himself.

Abraham hurries Sarah to bake cakes from three measures of flour — that's enough bread for forty. He runs to the herd, selects a tender calf, and stands while they eat under the tree. The patriarch waits on his guests himself.

Abraham hurries Sarah to bake cakes from three measures of flour — that's enough bread for forty. He runs to the herd, selects a tender calf, and stands while they eat under the tree. The patriarch waits on his guests himself.
Where is Sarah your wife? In the tent. And then the announcement: I will surely return at this time next year, and Sarah will have a son. Sarah is listening at the tent door.
Where is Sarah your wife? In the tent. And then the announcement: I will surely return at this time next year, and Sarah will have a son. Sarah is listening at the tent door.

Sarah and Abraham are old, past the age of childbearing. Sarah laughs to herself — after I am waxed old, shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also? Her laugh is honest. She doesn't dare voice it out loud.

Sarah and Abraham are old, past the age of childbearing. Sarah laughs to herself — after I am waxed old, shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also? Her laugh is honest. She doesn't dare voice it out loud.

But the LORD hears the silent laugh. Why did Sarah laugh? Is anything too hard for the LORD? Sarah denies it — she was afraid. He gently corrects: nay, but thou didst laugh. Even our secret doubts are seen.

But the LORD hears the silent laugh. Why did Sarah laugh? Is anything too hard for the LORD? Sarah denies it — she was afraid. He gently corrects: nay, but thou didst laugh. Even our secret doubts are seen.

But the LORD hears the silent laugh. Why did Sarah laugh? Is anything too hard for the LORD? Sarah denies it — she was afraid. He gently corrects: nay, but thou didst laugh. Even our secret doubts are seen.

The men rise to leave. God speaks to Himself: shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do? He has chosen Abraham not just for one nation, but to teach his children to walk in righteousness. The covenant has a teaching purpose.

The cry of Sodom and Gomorrah has reached me. I will go down to see. The men leave toward Sodom, but Abraham stays behind, standing before the LORD. The text pauses on his still posture — he is about to intercede.

The cry of Sodom and Gomorrah has reached me. I will go down to see. The men leave toward Sodom, but Abraham stays behind, standing before the LORD. The text pauses on his still posture — he is about to intercede.

The cry of Sodom and Gomorrah has reached me. I will go down to see. The men leave toward Sodom, but Abraham stays behind, standing before the LORD. The text pauses on his still posture — he is about to intercede.

Abraham draws near and begins the boldest prayer in Genesis: wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked? That be far from thee. Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right? He doesn't accuse — he appeals to God's character.

Abraham draws near and begins the boldest prayer in Genesis: wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked? That be far from thee. Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right? He doesn't accuse — he appeals to God's character.

Abraham draws near and begins the boldest prayer in Genesis: wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked? That be far from thee. Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right? He doesn't accuse — he appeals to God's character.
If I find fifty righteous, says God, I will spare the place. Abraham presses: what if there are forty-five? God answers: I will not destroy it for forty-five.
If I find fifty righteous, says God, I will spare the place. Abraham presses: what if there are forty-five? God answers: I will not destroy it for forty-five.
If I find fifty righteous, says God, I will spare the place. Abraham presses: what if there are forty-five? God answers: I will not destroy it for forty-five.
He continues to press: what if forty? Thirty? God agrees each time. Abraham is praying down a hostile city verse by verse.
He continues to press: what if forty? Thirty? God agrees each time. Abraham is praying down a hostile city verse by verse.
What if twenty? What if ten? God says, for the sake of ten I will not destroy it. Abraham stops at ten — perhaps thinking surely Lot's household alone is ten.
What if twenty? What if ten? God says, for the sake of ten I will not destroy it. Abraham stops at ten — perhaps thinking surely Lot's household alone is ten.
And the LORD went His way, and Abraham returned to his place. The conversation ends. The judgment is suspended on a single threshold of mercy: ten righteous.
